data.title: Oxford Lane Capital: Yes, You Can Fool Some People All The Time
data.description:
Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:OXLC) is very close to cutting its distribution. This is despite the "analysts" hanging on to the usual platitudes that come from the fund's press releases. In our last coverage, we called OXLC an American Pie In The Sky. The reference was to its yield, which stands virtually unmatched in terms of size and recruiting patsies. We have approached the problem of convincing the public in the most logical manner possible. We have used OXLC's numbers to show that you made less than 7.2% annually in OXLC if you reinvested distributions. The same slide showed that if you did not reinvest, you made something pitiful. That latter annualized number is so bad that we won't say it aloud (hint, the $1,262 number below), as it might boost antidepressant sales.

But today, we will go a very different route to show you something else about the fund.

The Covered Distribution

How many times have you heard it? The distribution is "covered". This stems from checking the quarterly press releases from the company and focusing on their coverage metrics. We are about to show you just how flawed that approach is. Over the last decade, the fund has used a few different terms in its press releases. These include,

1) GAAP Net Investment Income (September 2018 onwards).

2) Net Investment Income (from at least June 2014 until September 2018).

3) Estimated distributable net investment income (until December 2015).

4) Core Net Investment Income (March 2016 until today).

These are all shown per share/unit.

Of course, the standard is to benchmark one of these against the distribution/dividend paid around the same timeframe. We did the same, and you can find the distribution aligned against the Core Net Investment Income (CNII) below. We used CNII initially as it was the most consistent metric and one that intuitively makes you go, "If that covers the dividend, we should be fine."

Simple, right?

But look at what that reveals over the last decade. CNII was a cumulative $4.10 higher than the distributions since late 2015. Note that for two quarters, we used a slightly different metric mentioned in the press releases.

Does that not look peachy? Such a high income that it consistently covered the distribution? There is only one real problem here. The price and the NAV have dropped from around $13.88 in June 2015 all the way to below $4.50. The latest NAV is between $4.17-$4.27.

The price has followed.

So here, you have a metric that shows you that the distribution was completely covered over time. It showed you that you should have an NAV accretion of $4.10. So, with your starting NAV of $13.88 in June 2015, logic would dictate your ending NAV would be close to $18.00 if you believed this metric.

After all, everything was covered, and you had $4.10 extra. Why is the ending NAV almost $10.00 per unit lower? This is despite issuing countless shares at a premium, all of which is NAV accretive. Let's look at the other metrics we gathered. This time, we subtracted the distribution from GAAP Net Investment Income or Net Investment Income, which was present in the press release. Here, it appears fairly clear the distribution was not covered and exceeded our metric by about $2.00 per share.

Nonetheless, it fails to account for the large NAV drop over and above this. NAV should have been more than double if this metric showed true coverage. More importantly, it fails to address how this happened despite so much accretive share issuance.

Verdict

We are unsure which is the best metric to gauge the real-time coverage of the distribution. What we do know is that total returns are the only metric that will tell you how much you are making in the long run. Even on this, we would ignore the numbers put by automatic calculators as they assume distribution reinvestment and also assume no taxes on those large payouts. Individual tracking is the only way to go for such high yielders, which chronically deplete NAV.

The distribution is now lined up to be cut as the spread between GAAP Net Investment Income and distributions has become quite large.

OXLC gets the maximum risk of a cut fairly soon on our proprietary Kenny Loggins scale.

The big risk is that this moves to a discount to NAV as yield chasers finally look around the table and realize who is being played.

Our recommendation?

Stick with secondary securities like Oxford Lane Capital Corp. CAL 32 (NASDAQ:OXLCG), which protect your capital and still give you a very high real yield.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Buy MARA Holdings For Its Bitcoin And Get The Mining Business 'For Free'
data.description:
I have lately been quite interested in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Treasury Companies, both in terms of how these companies can obtain fiat capital on favorable terms to generate shareholder value, and in the potential opportunities to buy many of them at a discount relative to their BTC holdings and underlying operations. Regarding the first category, I have recently covered Strategy (MSTR), and each of its preferreds STRF (STRF), STRK (STRK), and, most recently, STRD (STRD), here, here, here, and here, respectively. Regarding the second category, back in March, I wrote how one can buy Semler Scientific's (SMLR) operations at a great discount relative to its BTC holdings. By the way, I think that opportunity is even greater now, given BTC's rally against SMLR's underperformance.

Today, I am writing about an opportunity like the latter, as MARA Holdings (NASDAQ:MARA) appears to trade at levels that allow investors to essentially acquire its mining business "for free."

The Bitcoin Holdings vs. MARA's Market Cap

As of writing this article, MARA's market cap sits around $5.8 billion, based on a share price of roughly $16.52 and about 352 million shares outstanding. In the meantime, the company holds 49,375 Bitcoin, valued at approximately $5.42 billion, with BTC flirting with $110,000 per coin.

If we do the math, that's about $15.41 in Bitcoin per share. Compare that to the stock's $16.52 price, and you're basically buying MARA's entire operating business (data centers, proprietary tech, etc.) for just about $1.1 per share, or $387 million. But wait! MARA has $2.44 billion in net debt (including the zero-coupon convertible notes), and it also has $1.56 billion in property and equipment, so by adding the differences as a liability, the market is valuing the mining business at just about $850 million. I will explain why this implies a discount in a moment.

The valuation discount has widened as MARA's stock hasn't kept pace with BTC's rally. While Bitcoin has gradually gained in recent months, MARA's share price has more or less moved sideways. BTC is up ~58% over the past year, all while MARA stock is down ~15% over the same period.

But why?

The Management Discount: Why Investors Are Skeptical

From following the stock regularly and reading the comments of other online investors, MARA's discount appears to stem from a doubt about management. Investors are essentially slapping a "management discount" on MARA, due to doubts about its track record, and it all goes back quite some time. You may remember, for instance, that in 2022, power outages at its Montana facility and delays in scaling mining capacity were a major source of frustration for shareholders.

On top of that, the $2 billion stock offering in March wasn't exactly welcomed by investors due to earlier fears of dilution, while the company's pivot into AI infrastructure raised questions about management's focus. And these are valid arguments. If MARA is now a BTC treasury company and raising money MSTR-like, why are they suddenly saying they want to explore becoming a base layer infrastructure provider for AI and high-performance computing?

Adding fuel to the fire, some investors have called out CEO Fred Thiel, pointing to his vague communication. For example, in the Q1 earnings call, he dodged specifics on AI strategy.

This has led the market to undervalue MARA's mining operations, pricing the stock as if its Bitcoin stash is pretty much its only asset.

The Mining Business Is Worth... The BTC

However, I believe the mining business is not worthless, or at least the mere $850 million implied book value (for MARA to trade at NAV). This is because of all the BTC accumulation that the mining operations deliver, which is profitable for the company. Last quarter, MARA mined 2,286 Bitcoin, down 19% from 2,811 the year before due to the halving and increased network difficulty. At a direct energy cost of $35,728 as of Q1 (ex-depreciation), MARA can make roughly $74,300 per BTC at current BTC prices. This is about $170 million in value creation in a single quarter. Note that MARA is actually going to post a loss in this scenario, as it incurs the expenses but holds the BTC, but book value-wise, it has created actual value.

Last month, MARA's mining produced 950 Bitcoin, a 35% jump from April, thanks to a record 282 blocks won and a 58.3 EH/s hashrate. At $110,000 per coin, those 950 Bitcoins are worth $104.5 million. With a $35,728 cost per Bitcoin, each coin adds $74,300 in book value, or $71 million for the month, or $852 million annualized.

I believe the market doesn't understand this and merely sees MARA's GAAP losses. You can also see how the mining business seems extremely cheaply priced, given the value MARA can create via its BTC accumulation strategy from its mining business. Within a year, the mined BTC minus the expenses incurred can top what the market is currently valuing the mining business. Even if you believe the balance sheet assets do not reflect real values on the equipment side, and the market is actually valuing the mining business at $1 billion to $1.5 billion, you are still getting it about 1X to 2X earnings at current Bitcoin price levels.

A Significant Risk To Consider

The most notable risk to MARA's investment case hinges on the price of BTC, which has a wild history. BTC has tanked by over 80% from its highs more than once. So, despite growing institutional adoption that could prevent such a major drop today, another crash could make mining unprofitable if Bitcoin falls below MARA's cost per coin, slashing balance sheet value and making its core operations effectively worthless for as long as mining remains unprofitable. Along with MARA's $2.44 billion in net debt, this could hammer the stock.

The Bottom Line

Overall, I would say it's tough to be bearish on MARA stock these days. You're essentially buying $5.42 billion in Bitcoin for $5.8 billion and getting a mining business that added $852 million in annualized Bitcoin value to the balance sheet for just $850 million.

Of course, the annualized figure can change from month to month, and the mining business could be worth anywhere between $0.50 and $1.5 billion at current share prices based on how much you think the balance sheet assets are actually worth. Still, the idea is the same, as we are talking about a low single-digit multiple on that business at current BTC prices.

In the meantime, if you are actually long BTC, you have another reason to hold the stock, even if the mining discount doesn't close. Since the stock is not trading far from its BTC holdings, you are holding the BTC and essentially getting a call option that the market will value the mining business above today's valuation.

Lastly, regarding the potential pivot toward AI, you have to trust that the management will indeed allocate power only if that is more accretive than actually mining BTC. It's all speculative to comment on that today, but if we assume that management doesn't mess up here, nothing fundamentally changes in my thesis.

data.title: Talen: Up Almost 400%, What's Built Into Today's Stock Price?
data.description:
Who is Talen?

Talen Energy (NASDAQ:TLN) was formed in 2015 when PPL Corporation (PPL) spun out its competitive generation business, and it merged with the competitive generation business of Riverstone Holdings, a private equity firm. Eventually, Riverstone took the entire company private, but in 2022 they were forced to declare bankruptcy.

The bulk of Talen’s generation fleet is in the PJM power market. The most important asset by far is the Susquehanna Nuclear Facility. Susquehanna has over 2,200MW of capacity and is one of the most efficient nuclear power plants in the country.

Exhibit 1

What’s Happened at Talen

When Talen emerged from bankruptcy in June 2023, its stock started trading at around $46/share. Today, the stock is over $270/share, and it is closing in on a 400% increase in about two years' time.

Exhibit 2

Talen’s stock price started to increase as the market realized that the surge in AI demand would have a positive impact on electricity generators. Things really started to take off for Talen when on March 4, 2024, they announced a deal with Amazon Web Services (AWS) (A subsidiary of Amazon (AMZN)).

Exhibit 3

Prior to bankruptcy, Talen had created a digital campus subsidiary adjacent to the Susquehanna nuclear plant. On 3/4/24 AWS agreed to buy this asset and to purchase power from Susquehanna at attractive prices.

Exhibit 4

The deal with AWS created a lot of value for Talen. It implied AWS buying electricity at approximately $80/MWh, well above current price levels. As shown in Exhibit 4, this agreement was projected to add $80M of additional revenue in 2026, and it could potentially add up to $255M in 2034 and beyond. The announcement signaled to investors that data center operators were willing to pay a premium for reliable, clean energy.

Beyond the AWS deal, the broader electricity market also began to provide tailwinds. A pivotal moment came in July 2024, when the PJM 2025-26 capacity market auction cleared at a record high of $269.92/MW-day. This represented nearly a tenfold increase compared to the previous year’s auction. (For those who don’t know, PJM basically runs the electric transmission grid from New Jersey to Chicago and south into Virginia.) (I’ve written previously about the PJM capacity auction here.)

Exhibit 5

For those who might not be aware, the capacity auction essentially pays power plants a standby fee to be available on peak demand days. Without these payments, it would be hard to justify keeping plants that only run a few hours a year because they wouldn’t operate enough to recover their costs.

Talen had 6,820MW clear in the 25/26 auction, which will provide Talen with over $670M in revenue assuming all their plants operate properly during the time period. (365 days x 6,620MW x $270/MW-day)

One of the big reasons for the higher capacity prices was PJM’s increased power demand forecast from the previous year.

Exhibit 6

The rise in the forecasted demand is coming from several places. The outlook for new data centers is a big driver, but the rise in electric vehicles, the electrification of more residential demand, and an increase in industrialization are also pulling the forecast higher.

This week, the upside catalysts continued, with Talen announcing an update to their AWS agreement. They doubled the amount of power that AWS will eventually take, consuming almost all of Susquehanna’s output, and they changed the structure of the agreement to reduce some of the regulatory issues that have plagued the deal since it was first announced. They have also agreed to collaborate on potential uprates of Susquehanna’s capacity and the possibility of bringing small modular reactors to Susquehanna’s site.

Calculating Talen’s Value

So, Talen’s stock price obviously has some good reasons for the increase, now the question is, has it gone too far?

Talen’s 2024 year-end results presentation shows a huge upcoming increase in free cash flow in 2026.

Exhibit 7

This week’s presentation showed even more potential upside when looking at just the updated AWS deal.

Exhibit 8

Using the 2026 numbers from Exhibit 7 and today’s stock price of approximately $270/share, you get an FCF yield of 4.3-7.2%. This valuation range suggests that the stock is neither obviously cheap nor prohibitively expensive.

To gain a clearer understanding, I modeled Talen’s operations in detail, starting with the Susquehanna power plant, their most valuable asset. The 2024 Investor Day presentation gives some important inputs.

Exhibit 9

Current power prices in PJM are around the $50/MWh level.

Exhibit 10

Putting all these assumptions together has Susquehanna generating about $700M of EBITDA per year.

Exhibit 11

I next looked at Talen’s four major gas plants. The 2024 Investor Day presentation gave the heat rate (a measure of fuel efficiency) for these plants, and it gave a forecast of the combined output of these four plants plus the much smaller Camden plant.

Exhibit 12

The investor day presentation did not provide information on the capacity factor at the individual plants, or on the plants’ non-fuel operating costs, but I was able to use historical data from S&P Capital IQ Pro to fill in the gaps.

Exhibit 13

Based on these assumptions, the four PJM gas plants generate just over $600M of annual EBITDA.

In addition to these core assets, Talen recently signed reliability must-run agreements with PJM for two older coal plants, Brandon Shores and Wagner. These agreements delay the planned shutdown of the plants and provide fixed payments of $145 million and $35 million per year, respectively, along with reimbursement for variable operating expenses.

Talen also owns partial stakes in the Keystone and Conemaugh coal plants in western Pennsylvania. Talen has provided limited financial data on these assets, but with information from S&P Capital IQ Pro, I was able to create some reasonable estimates.

Exhibit 14

Combining company guidance for CAPEX, depreciation, and interest expense — along with a slight increase in G&A expense and a 30% overall tax rate — the company appears capable of generating over $700M of FCF. This is over $15.50/share, and at a stock price of $270, it gives an FCF yield of 5.75%.

Exhibit 15

I conducted a sensitivity analysis to understand how changes in PJM capacity prices and the price of energy from Susquehanna might impact Talen’s valuation.

Exhibit 16

The highlighted cell shows the values used in my model. As noted earlier, the latest capacity auction came in almost 10x higher than the previous auction. If future auctions were to go back to clearing at that low level, it would obviously be a significant blow to FCF, but the company would likely remain FCF-positive. Importantly, this risk seems to have been eliminated for the next two years. PJM and FERC (the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission) have agreed to a floor of $175/MW-day and a cap of $325/MW-day in the next two auctions. If the increase in electricity demand forecasted in Exhibit 6 comes to pass, the outlook for capacity auctions would likely be at the higher end of historical values.

The impact of Susquehanna energy prices seems to be similar; a large drop in prices would hurt FCF, but it would mostly still be positive. There are also factors protecting against this downside. The Nuclear Production Tax Credit supports nuclear power plants if the revenue received falls below $43.75/MWh. With the credit, even if power prices were to go to $0/MWh, Talen would still receive $15/MWh for their Susquehanna output. Exhibit 17 gives more information about the PTC.

Exhibit 17

As mentioned earlier, the AWS agreement seems to imply a price of $80/MWh, so it seems like the odds favor an increase in the Susquehanna energy price. Also, with this week’s update, almost all of Susquehanna’s capacity could end up at this higher rate. During this week’s conference call, management also said there are inflation adjustments over time, which will help if the cost of operating Susquehanna were to increase. Also, if the demand for electricity continues to increase as forecasted, there should be a general upward trend in prices that could help if the AWS deal ever fell apart.

Other factors to consider:

  • Index Inclusion: Talen will soon be eligible for indexes like the S&P 400, potentially increasing share demand.
  • Regulatory risks: Although the revised AWS deal addresses past concerns, regulatory uncertainty hasn’t been fully eliminated. The vast forecasted increases in electric demand could lead to new regulations.
  • Asset concentration: Susquehanna is by far the biggest piece of Talen’s value. An operational issue (e.g., a major outage) could have a disproportionate impact through higher cost replacement power and/or large amounts of CAPEX.
  • Share buybacks: Talen is opportunistically repurchasing shares. In Q1, they bought back 452,000 shares at an average price below $185, even though the stock was above $200 for most of the quarter. These purchases will have a positive effect on per-share FCF going forward.

Conclusion

Despite a nearly 400% run-up since 2023, Talen may still offer an upside. The 5.75% FCF yield from my model does not give you a cheap stock, but it also doesn't give a big sell signal either. As the many catalysts in the power industry play out, there is a good chance further upward movement in the stock will be justified. But, these changes will take time, and stock increases at the rate of the last two years should not be expected. This week’s updated AWS agreement is a good sign for these positive trends, but if things don’t turn out as well as hoped, there are a lot of factors that will provide downside protection.

Modeling Note:

Sharp-eyed readers may notice that I modeled some plants using a $50/MWh price, and for others, I used a $55/MWh price. The plants where I used the higher price were plants that had a much lower capacity factor. The lower capacity factor means the plant runs fewer hours, so the model assumes that the hours the plants aren’t running are lower cost hours. With the lower-cost hours eliminated, this will increase the average price received by the power plant.

data.title: The Market Is Built Upon A 'House Of Cards'
data.description:
Markets have staged an impressive rebound since their mid-April lows. Equities have become quite impervious to any bad news and the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) is right near all-time highs after briefly entering official bear market territory on the huge sell-off after reciprocal tariffs were announced on April 2nd. While myriad trade negotiations are ongoing, there have been precious little new trade deals announced outside a new trade agreement with the United Kingdom. Equities also seem unbothered by the ongoing conflict in Ukraine, the falling dollar or the recent unrest in Los Angeles.

Stocks by several valuation metrics (Price-to-sales ratio, market cap-to-GDP ratio, Shiller P/E) are deep in overbought territory. Investors seem content to ignore these flashing yellow lights even as the "risk-free" yield on the 10-Year Treasury (US10Y) hovers near 4.5%. However, to me, the markets are balancing on a "House of Cards." This existential factor is also likely to be the trigger for the next big downturn in the markets. That issue is simply the massive amount of debt out there as interest rates remain higher for longer. Let's break this debt into three categories: Sovereign, Real Estate, and Consumer.

Sovereign Debt:

There is an 800lb gorilla in the room in my opinion. That is sovereign debt. The United States has gone from around $6 trillion in federal debt at the start of the 21st century to over $36 trillion now. The cost to service the current debt is now north of $1 trillion annually, and the second-largest line item in the federal budget after Social Security. The nation's debt-to-GDP ratio is right at all-time highs. Obviously, this is an existential issue that will need to be addressed at some point either with higher taxes and/or lower government spending. Either of which would be a significant headwind to economic growth.

Unfortunately, the U.S. is not the only major country drowning in debt. Japan's debt-to-GDP ratio is now north of 250% in a country which has worse demographics (lower birth rate, higher median age, etc.) and imports most of its energy needs. Adding in regional governmental debt, China's debt-to-GDP ratio hovers around 300% and the country has the same demographic and energy challenges as Japan.

In Europe, the largest economy, Germany has a management debt-to-GDP ratio of around 65%. However, the country has had worse demographics and slower growth than the United States. In addition, Europe is about to massively increase their defense spending in the coming years. This will ratchet up debt-to-GDP ratios across the continent.

Ending on a brighter note, India which has the largest population in the world has a debt-to-GDP ratio of under 60%. The country also has much better demographics than China, but much poorer infrastructure. The country also imports the majority of its energy needs.

Real Estate Debt:

The good news here is that because of reforms made after the GFC, there are few liar loans or much mortgage application fraud to blow up the residential real estate market like during the Housing Bust. Most home mortgages are made with at least a 20% down payment. Many homes are purchased with cash only. In 2024, nearly one-third of all U.S. home sales were all cash. There is a huge amount of equity that is in the housing market thanks to the large amount of home appreciation over the past several years. Outside of FHA loans (a small percentage of overall mortgages) that require low down payments, it is hard to see a delinquency or foreclosure problem on the horizon.

That said, housing affordability is near all-time lows. Home inventories are also piling up. In May, inventories were up some 23% compared to the same month in 2024. This will be an economic headwind as 15% to 18% of overall economic activity in the U.S. is powered by the currently moribund housing sector. This obviously will also continue to cloud the outlook for homebuilding stocks as margins get increasingly squeezed in order to move growing inventory levels. During the height of the pandemic-driven housing boom in 2022, large homebuilder Lennar Corporation (LEN) cost for incentives like free upgrades, mortgage rate buy downs, and "Flexcash" ran at 1.5% of overall sales. In Q1, that incentive percentage was around 13%.

The problem on the real estate side is the commercial real estate sector. The problems around the office sector have been well documented at this point. The delinquency rate on Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS, on office properties has surged to north of 10% from under two percent a few years ago. And that doesn't count loans where creditors are currently engaging in "extend and pretend" behavior. More office space will be demolished and converted than built this year, for the first time in a quarter century. This obviously is another economic headwind. One reason construction spending overall fell in May from April.

It is not only delinquency rates on office properties moving higher. CMBS delinquency rates for Lodging and Retail are north of six percent as is on Multi-Family properties, which has more than tripled over the past 12 months. In September of last year, it was estimated that $2 trillion of CRE debt would need to be refinanced at much higher rates over the next three years. U.S. community and regional banks are almost five times more exposed to the CRE sector than big banks it should be noted.

Consumer Debt:

Then we have the situation around consumer debt, which is deteriorating. Auto loan delinquencies to subprime consumers have reached the highest level ever measured according to Fitch.

U.S. Credit Card debt has spiked in recent years as well and stands near a record of just under $1.2 trillion.

Then we have U.S. student debt which stood at nearly $1.8 trillion with just over 42 million borrowers at the end of the fourth quarter. After a five-year taxpayer-funded hiatus, initially due to the Covid pandemic, student loan payments and the default process have been restarted. This will be an increasing consumer headwind in the quarters and years ahead.

Conclusion:

So, while the market is focused on other issues like tariff and trade policies, inflation, Federal Reserve monetary policy, the falling dollar, etc., my longer-term focus is the debt situation. It is hard to see how this resolves itself without dire consequences for the economy and therefore the markets. The rise in equities feels like it is built on a "house of cards" on a longer-term basis.

Therefore, while roughly 75% of my portfolio is still allocated to equities, this allocation is within covered call holdings around the few stocks I have found in the market that still sports a reasonable valuation. The other quarter is in short-term treasuries that will act as my "dry powder" should markets have another deep sell-off like we had in the first two weeks of April (which seems to already have been forgotten by investors and market pundits alike).

data.title: GigaCloud's Floor Has Materialized -- Speculative And Slow Recovery Ahead
data.description:
GCT's Floor Triggers An Improved Margin Of Safety Amongst The Uncertainties

We previously covered GigaCloud Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:GCT) in April 2025, discussing why it had massively underperformed since the 2024 heights, attributed to the macroeconomic/geopolitical uncertainties.

Combined with the mixed FQ1'25 revenue guidance, the potential growth headwinds arising from the tariff headwinds, and the elevated short interest, we had rated the stock as a Hold (Neutral) then, despite the oversold status, the attractive valuations, and the rich balance sheet.

GCT 1Y Stock Price

Since then, GCT has further retraced by -10% to find a floor at the $11 range while rapidly recovering by +66.5% since then, proving our bearish thesis wrong, with a similar stock price trend also observed in its diversified commerce peers in varying degrees.

Much of the recovery tailwinds are attributed to the 90-day tariff pause and the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US and China, underscoring why sentiments surrounding consumer discretionary stocks have lifted, despite the slowing consumer spending trends.

This is significantly aided by GCT's robust performance metrics in FQ1'25, with the increasing active 3P sellers to 1,154 (+3.6% QoQ/+33.4% YoY) and the active buyers to 9,966 (+7% QoQ/+81.4% YoY) already triggering the growing GigaCloud Marketplace Gross Merchandise Value to $1.41B (+5.2% QoQ/ +56.1% YoY) for the 12 months ended March 31, 2025.

These underscore the growing adoption of its marketplace offerings/ services, especially in the EU, where revenues are growing exponentially to $57.33M (+47.9% QoQ/+83.2% YoY), with it underscoring the management's successful diversification to different geographical regions.

This is significantly aided by GCT's expanding marketplace/non-standard bulky offerings across furniture, auto parts, fitness equipment, larger toys, gardening tools, pet supplies, and bathtubs.

This is on top of its successful BaaS program, as it reports growing partnerships with more brands and sellers/ distributors, with it already flowing to the growing overall service revenues of $94.06M (-3.1% QoQ/ +22.7% YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

On the other hand, there is no denying the drastic deceleration in GCT's growth prospects, based on the management's mixed FQ2'25 revenue guidance of $290M at the midpoint (+6.6% QoQ/-6.7% YoY) compared to a year ago at +23.8% QoQ/+103.1% YoY.

While the management has highlighted their intensified efforts to grow their business beyond the US, there is no denying the fact that the country comprises $190.62M (-5.1% YoY) or the equivalent of 70.1% of its revenues (-9.9 points YoY) — with it implying moderate headwinds from H2'25 onwards, once the tariff impacts flow through.

As a result, it is unsurprising why the consensus has already lowered their forward estimates, with GCT expected to chart a top/bottom-line performance at a 2Y CAGR of +3.7%/ -2.9%, down drastically from the original estimates of +19.6%/+24.1% and the 3Y historical growth profile at +41%/+51.3%, respectively.

While demand for furniture has seemingly picked up, as similarly reported by Home Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW) in the recent earnings call, it is apparent that the home buying activities remain soft, with it remaining to be seen when consumer demand for large furniture/ home appliance may normalize to healthier levels.

Combined with the likely higher for longer interest rates environment, we believe that GCT is only suitable for investors with a higher risk tolerance and longer investing trajectory.

So, Is GCT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GCT 3Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have already defended GCT's floor at the $11s range by early April 2025, as the stock also recovers to retest its resistance levels/200-day moving averages of the $18s range.

GCT Valuations

Despite the recent recovery, GCT remains extremely discounted at our estimated TTM P/E non-GAAP valuations of 4.94x, based on the stock prices of $18.60 by the time of writing and the LTM adj EPS of $3.76 (-8% sequentially).

The same conclusion may be observed against our estimated FY2026 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 6.73x, based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $2.76, when compared to the sector median of 17.17x and its diversified commerce peers, including Amazon (AMZN) at 34.99x, MercadoLibre (MELI) at 48.12x, and Sea Limited (SE) at 41.09x.

Despite GCT's supposedly stagnant growth profile, it goes without saying that its eventual re-rating in valuations may trigger a great upside potential for opportunistic investors, with an estimated intermediate-term price target of $55.20, based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $2.76 and its 3Y P/E mean of ~20x (still cheap compared to its peer group mean of ~29x).

Its prospects are significantly aided by its (still) positive Free Cash Flow generation at $7.04M in FQ1'25 (-37.7% YoY) and $142.54M in FY2024 (+10.4% YoY), along with the healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $251.71M (-3% QoQ/ +35.9% YoY) and the inherent lack of debt, implying its ability to weather the near-term demand/ macro headwinds.

This is on top of the ongoing shareholder return with -3% of its float retired since FQ2'24, as the management also takes advantage of the stock's cheap valuations.

Does this mean that we are re-rating the GCT stock to a Buy here?

A cautious yes, with numerous caveats.

Interested investors may want to observe the GCT stock price movement for a little longer, preferably adding nearer to the established bottom of $11s ranges for an improved margin of safety, given its drastically higher short interest volumes by +49% on a YoY basis, with the recent gains potentially moderated by aggressive short sellers.

At the same time, there is no denying the fluidity of the ongoing trade negotiations, with it remaining to be seen if the tariffs may be fully lifted or be a new normal.

Despite the long-awaited emergence of a trading floor (after the -71.7% stock price decline between the May 2024 peaks and April 2025 bottom), we recommend investors temper their expectations, since the stock may also underperform in the near-term despite the bright long-term prospects.

data.title: Newly Listed GBank Financial Holdings Has An Edge In Fintech
data.description:
On April 30, GBank Financial Holdings (NASDAQ:GBFH) began trading on the NASDAQ, moving up from the OTCQX marketplace. The NASDAQ listing followed a banner year and a half for the company, which has seen its stock go from $9.90 at the start of December 2023 to its current price of just under $40 per share. While the stock is not a household name to most people outside of its home base of Las Vegas, a 300% gain in less than 18 months and a 149.4% gain in the last 12 deserves a deeper look.

What happened in December 2023 that kick-started the stock’s meteoric rise? Part of the increase in the share price may be the added credibility that the company received from being listed on a major US exchange. However, the main culprit may be the company’s decision to buy BankCard Services, LLC, a fintech company that provides cashless payment options to gaming apps. Before this announcement, the company was largely known only for providing traditional banking services to both business and consumer clients. It was also an active facilitator of Small Business Administration loans.

With the recent run-up in share price, the bank's valuation has started to look more like a tech play than a regional bank. After looking through the financial data for the company, I think the benefits of owning GBFH at this time are about equal to its risks. I am rating the stock as a hold for now, but I have to admit that this company is certainly one to watch in the months ahead for the reasons that I will discuss here.

Company Snapshot

GBank Financial Holdings is the parent company of GBank, a regional bank in Nevada with only two local branches in the metro Las Vegas area. GBank was originally named the Bank of George, with homage to the country’s first president, when it was founded in 2007. The company offers the typical community banking services to its customers in Southern Nevada.

The company also has a national footprint with two additional business activities – SBA lending and Gaming Fintech. According to the company website, GBank’s fintech team uses “proprietary banking solutions to achieve FDIC-insured accounts for major gaming company customers." The bank's services include offering Pooled Player and Prepaid Accounts that address funding challenges that are unique to the gaming industry. GBank also offers its Visa Signature® Card, which is a solution tailored to meet the needs of gaming and sports entertainment consumers.

At the end of its last quarter on March 31, GBFH reported total assets of $1.19 billion. The company controlled $996 million in deposits and a loan portfolio, after allowances for credit losses, of $834 million. Deposits had grown 6.5% from Q4 2024. Loan growth during that same time period reached 3.4%. The company earned $1.41 per share in FY 2024. That was a 64% improvement from FY 2023.

A snapshot of GBank’s loan portfolio from the company’s most recent earnings report is given below. Non-owner-occupied commercial real estate takes up the lion’s share of the loan book at $672.4 million. That is 79.7% of the entire total. The next closest category is owner-occupied CRE at $81.8 million or 9.7%.

The company currently pays no dividend, which is somewhat unusual for a financial company. The company made no mention of a future dividend in its most recent 10-Q.

Investment Analysis for GBFH

On May 28, GBank Financial Holdings filed its latest 10-Q. EPS of $0.31 per share in Q1 2025 was a $0.06 decrease from the last earnings report but a $0.02 improvement from the same quarter last year. The company is projected to earn $1.75 this year, a 24.1% improvement from 2024. Earnings in 2026 are expected to be $2.37, which would be a 35.4% gain and mark the third straight year of profit growth of over 24%.

The profit numbers are not the whole story with GBank. There are a couple of specific areas where the bank’s strength really stands out. The first is the bank’s net interest margin and low cost of deposits. For the three months ended on March 31, GBFH had an NIM of 4.47%. While that is a decline from the same quarter last year, that number is still 90 basis points ahead of the national average for a bank of its size. GBank also had non-interest-bearing deposits of $219 million, or 23.4% of its total deposits, which helps keep its cost of deposits down.

There has been a slight uptick in the company’s non-performing assets recently, and its loan portfolio with an abundance of non-owner-occupied CRE certainly comes with elevated risks. NPAs at the end of Q1 2025 totaled $20.4 million, which was 1.71% of the bank’s total assets. At the end of Q4 2024, that number was only $14.2 million. However, the company’s leverage ratio made a significant improvement in the last quarter, rising from 12.9% on December 31, 2024, to 14.23% on March 31 of this year. Non-performing assets, when guaranteed portions from the SBA program are removed, represented only 0.48% of total assets at the end of the last quarter.

Below is a comparison of several key financial metrics for GBank and its peers as listed by Seeking Alpha. The other five banks listed here are as follows: SmartFinancial (SMBK), Eagle Bancorp (EGBN), Washington Trust Bancorp (WASH), Capital Bancorp (CBNK), and Financial Institutions (FISI). In the final column, I included the national averages for these metrics for other regional banks.

GBFH

SMBK

At first glance, GBank appears to be overvalued, both when compared to a group of its peers, and also when compared to the national averages. However, its ROE and ROA numbers are significantly stronger than all comparisons. Those returns, in addition to the strong profit growth numbers, are what is keeping the stock from moving higher. Below is a comparison of the performance of the stocks from this group over the last year. While GBFH has some higher valuations, investors have been willing to pay up for this performance.

This comparison is also not a fair test of GBank's merits, as none of the other regional banks have the fintech component that GBFH offers, and none of them offer it in the city where it should be most valued.

GBank’s Fintech Advantage

A bank in Las Vegas would seem out of place if it were not somehow connected to the gaming industry. What makes the GBank Visa Signature® Card so unique and valuable for gamblers is that it treats their transactions like purchases instead of cash advances. When a user loads funds onto a sports or gaming app, the transaction avoids the additional fees that come with a cash advance or ATM withdrawal. Lower costs facilitate more transactions and lowers a barrier for participation.

There are additional advantages for GBank customers who use their newly acquired technology. The bank can also facilitate cash-free mobile payments for sports books and casinos. Speed of payouts is an especially important service for gamblers who want access to their winnings. Speed and ease of payment, of course, are equally important to the casino. The intellectual property that was acquired by GBank in its purchase of BankCard Services can also be licensed to other banks and financial service providers in the future for potential additional revenue streams.

There is no doubt that non-interest income ballooned in Q1 2025 when compared to the same quarter last year. At the heart of the expansion were net interchange fees, which can be attributed directly to GBank's credit card and mobile payment system. An annual increase of 127% in non-interest income is certainly noteworthy, and it seems like the bank is only starting to leverage its position in this market.

Gaming Industry Snapshot

The sports betting and gaming industry in the US is currently experiencing an extended period of rapid growth, boosted by an ever-expanding list of states that have legalized online betting. The number of users for online betting apps is also rising, with user penetration growing from 11% last year to almost 16% by 2029.

Revenue for online gaming, which would include sports betting, grew by over 15% in Q1 2025. According to the American Gaming Association, the combined revenue for the first three months of this year from online gaming and sports betting set a new record of $6.19 billion. Online gaming is growing even faster than online betting as it made up a record 32.8% of commercial gaming revenue in Q1, compared to 29.3% during the same period last year. GBank appears to be in the right place at the right time to take advantage of these trends.

Risks for Investing in GBFH

The company's exposure to non-owner-occupied commercial real estate is concerning. Repayment of these loans is often solely dependent on rental income, which can fluctuate for any number of reasons. The bank's non-performing assets are something to keep an eye on in future quarterly reports.

GBank is heavily dependent on the gaming industry and the state of Nevada for its overall financial health. The company has likely earned its current elevated valuation because of its connection to sports betting and mobile apps like DraftKings (DKNG), William Hill, and PokerStars. Should the gaming industry suffer a slowdown because of an overall decline in economic activity in the US or specific regulatory issues that impact its industry, GBFH could suffer. Troubles in the gaming industry could also impact the overall health of the Nevada economy, and Nevada is where GBank has over 20% of its loan portfolio.

Conclusion

While the fintech division certainly makes comparisons with other regional banks slightly less relevant, I do feel like the valuation for the company has become a little stretched in the last six months. I would like to see a few more quarters of results from GBank as a publicly traded company before I consider upgrading this stock to a buy.

Performance data quoted represent past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For most recent month-end performance information, visit www.impaxam.com. Figures include reinvested dividends, capital gains distribution, and changes in principal value.

1First full month since inception. The inception date for Impax Small Cap Fund Institutional Class is 03/27/2008 and Investor Class is 03/27/2008, and Class A is 05/01/2013. As of 05/01/2024 prospectus, total annual Impax Small Cap Fund operating expenses, gross of any fee waivers or reimbursements (excluding Acquired Fund fees and expenses), for Institutional Class, Investors Class, and Class A shares are 0.91%, 1.16% and 1.16%, respectively.

The performance information shown for Class A represents the performance of the Investor Class shares for the period prior to Class A inception. Expenses have not been adjusted to reflect the expenses allocable to Class A shares. Class A inception date return since May 1, 2013, is 8.27% (annualized). A 1.00% CDSC (contingent deferred sales charge) may be charged on any shares sold within 18 months of purchase over $1 million. POP (public offering price) reflects the maximum sales load for the Fund’s Class A shares of 5.50%.

Performance

The fund outperformed the benchmark during the last quarter.

Market overview

  • US small cap stocks, as measured by the Russell 2000, declined sharply in Q1 after early strength turned negative as uncertainty surrounding US tariffs dominated sentiment and news flow.
  • The continued broadening of markets drove a change in sector-level leadership with technology stocks lagging all sectors during the period. Following strong returns in January, incoming US President Donald Trump spooked markets with threats, retractions and the eventual implementation of tariffs on key trading partners. Amid the uncertainty, the US Federal Reserve held interest rates in both February and March and projected slower growth and higher core inflation by year end.
  • From a sector perspective, Energy was at the forefront of index returns as global oil prices rallied. Utilities and other defensive sectors also showed relative strength during the period. The pullback in technology continued as the sector delivered the weakest returns of the quarter followed by Consumer Discretionary stocks.
  • Looking beyond the short-term volatility triggered by Trump’s tariffs, we believe global growth is still on track to improve in 2026. As long-term investors, we aim to manage portfolios through this challenging period with near-term caution and optimism for the longer term.

Key performance drivers

The Small Cap portfolio outperformed its benchmark during a difficult period for small-cap stocks. The strategy benefited from defensive capital allocation at the sector level, although security selection was somewhat muted. While Consumer Discretionary was among the worst-performing sectors, the portfolio’s stock selection was strong. Leading contributions came from a new position in electric vehicle maker Rivian (RIVN), which delivered positive absolute returns, and Planet Fitness (PLNT), which beat analyst earnings estimates for same-store sales and reported better-than-expected penetration from its premium black card service. Selection was also additive in Real Estate, where the portfolio delivered positive absolute returns. Agree Realty (ADC), a low beta, high-quality retail REIT benefited from the heightened volatility witnessed in the first quarter. Rayonier (RYN), a timber REIT holding benefited from potential tariffs on softwood lumber. In contrast, the portfolio saw negative selection in Consumer Staples as Freshpet (FRPT) shares fell short of lofty expectations and provided a mixed outlook. Industrials also faced challenges with Generac (GNRC), a backup power solutions provider, posting decent fourth-quarter numbers but weighed down by conservative 2025 guidance.

Contributors

BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ)(Consumer Staples) rose on the back of strong fourth-quarter results, and some restrained optimism in the full-year guidance, with no expectations of a severe slowdown in the back half of the year. Same-store sales were up mid-single digits, well ahead of expectations. The company also benefits from relatively limited tariff risk which the market sees as constructive in a volatile environment for global trade. Pacira BioSciences (PCRX)(Health Care) shares jumped on news that several of the company’s key drugs delivered better-than- expected revenue growth in the fourth quarter. Included in this is the company’s non-opioid pain drug, Exparel, which the company hopes to deliver to 3 million patients by 2030. This would imply double-digit annualized growth. Merit Medical Systems (MMSI)(Health Care) which produces medical devices for life-saving or preserving procedures in critical fields such as cardiology and oncology, outperformed in the quarter as the company delivered organic revenue growth that was at the higher end of market expectations, and a material beat on earnings. Investors are particularly enthusiastic about Wrapsody, a medical device designed to help dialysis patients who experience complications due to blockages in their dialysis outflow.

Detractors

Health Catalyst (HCAT)(Health Care) reported Q4 results in line with expectations, with mid-single digit revenue growth year and strong expense management driving an improved bottom line. Despite positive end-market trends and new client wins, recent capital allocation decisions have disappointed investors, particularly the dilutive acquisition of Upfront Healthcare. The company’s management remains optimistic about accelerating revenue growth and profitability. Victory Capital (VCTR)(Financials) was weaker as the stock fell in line with other asset managers amid the broad market sell off. This weak price action unfolded despite strong fourth quarter results with earnings per share surpassing consensus by a notable margin. From a flow perspective, net outflows in the fourth quarter were significant, however, management did comment that January flows were positive, and the company had a record "won but not yet funded" book. The Amundi US acquisition is still on schedule, which will add a substantial amount of assets under management. Freshpet Inc (Consumer Staples) was lower in the period as investors priced in the risk of a slowdown. The company’s 2025 sales growth forecasts were below estimates and weighted towards the back half of the year. This weighed on the company’s shares – overshadowing a strong quarter from an earnings perspective – and management’s plan to drive attractive margin improvement by 2027.

Outlook

The new Trump administration’s approach to global trade has shifted dramatically from historical precedent, creating an atmosphere of uncertainty within risk markets. This volatility has had adverse effects on small-cap stocks as the likelihood of an economic recession has increased. However, in the long term, a focus on domestic trade is poised to benefit high-quality, domestically oriented small caps. Successful investment strategies must remain disciplined to be able to navigate uncertain periods and thrive during more favorable market conditions. As the impact of tariffs begins to abate, we anticipate an uptick in merger and acquisition activity, which should prove advantageous for small-cap stocks. The investment team continues to prioritize identifying attractive businesses that provide solutions to transformative health care challenges, resource efficiency, mobility, access to finance, and circular economy principles. We believe, investing in high-quality, low-risk stocks that align with these long-term secular growth trends will bolster portfolio resilience against market turbulence.

Fund overview

Impax Small Cap Fund is a core US equities strategy that invests in companies with attractive valuations and sustainability tailwinds, with the aim to achieve capital growth over 5+ years.

Portfolio managers

Nathan Moser CFA®, Diederik Basch CFA®, Curtis Kim CFA®

CFA® is a trademark owned by CFA Institute.

Definitions

Weighted Average is an average in which each quantity to be averaged is assigned a weight. These weightings determine the relative importance of each quantity on the average.

revealing how much profit a company generates with the money shareholders have invested.

∞

The Ex-Ante Beta is calculated using a multi-factor risk model. Beta explains common variations in stock returns due to different stock sensitivities to the market relative to its underlying benchmark for the current period, not historical. A beta for a benchmark is 1.00: A beta greater than 1.00 indicates above average volatility and risk.

Definitions and disclosures

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the US equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000 Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership.

One cannot invest directly in an index.

RISK: Equity investments are subject to market fluctuations, the fund’s share price can fall because of weakness in the broad market, a particular industry, or specific holdings. Funds that emphasize investments in smaller companies generally will experience greater price volatility. The Fund is actively managed. The investment techniques and decisions of the investment adviser and the Fund’s portfolio manager(s), including the investment adviser’s assessment of a company’s ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance) profile when selecting investments for the Fund, may not produce the desired results and may adversely impact the Fund’s performance, including relative to other Funds that do not consider ESG factors or come to different conclusions regarding such factors.

Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) criteria are a set of standards for a company’s operations that socially conscious investors use to screen potential investments. Environmental criteria consider how a company performs as a steward of nature. Social criteria examine how it manages relationships with employees, suppliers, customers, and the communities where it operates. Governance deals with a company’s leadership, executive pay, audits, internal controls, and shareholder rights.

The statements and opinions expressed are those of the author as of the date of this report. All information is historical and not indicative of future results and subject to change. data.title: Apple: Risk Of Falling Behind In AI Race Increases After Disappointing WWDC Event
data.description:
Apple Inc.’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) recent WWDC event has been a disappointment on the AI front. A CNBC headline summed it up perfectly by saying that the company failed to meet even the basic expectations. Wall Street has also delivered a dip in the stock price after the event.

Apple is the only major big tech player which has not made a big investment in AI infrastructure or services. Even Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), with its more modest cash flow compared to Apple, has been investing heavily in AI. Apple’s management is trying to be more prudent in its spending on AI, but it is possible that the company will fall significantly behind. A year ago, there was a lot of excitement around Apple Intelligence. Back then I mentioned that the company might not meet the hype due to lack of any major investments in this segment. We are already seeing a more modest upgrade cycle for the iPhone. This could continue in the next iteration, hurting the overall revenue and the brand. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Apple could continue to face headwinds in the international business, especially in China.

Apple’s wearables segment is showing a YoY decline in revenue despite the added revenue from Vision Pro. The company also faces challenges in the Services segment and recently lost its bid to stop App Store reform in the Epic Games order. Apple is trading at 25.30 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Sep 2026, which is quite high when we compare it to other big tech companies that have a better EPS growth momentum.

Apple risks falling behind significantly in the AI race

Apple has been a latecomer in many product and service segments in the past. However, it has been able to carve a good niche in many segments despite the late entry. The management might have similar views on AI. This has led to smaller and incremental investments in building AI services compared to the all-in approach by almost all the big tech companies.

The recent WWDC event was a big disappointment when we looked at the possible AI roadmap for Apple. There had been a lot of hype around Apple Intelligence a year ago. Many bullish analysts projected that this would start another big upgrade cycle for the iPhone and could open up new monetization options for the company. The hype has not met reality, and we have not seen any major upgrade trajectory in the iPhone in the last few quarters. In the recent quarter, Apple’s iPhone revenue was $46.8 billion compared to $45.9 billion in the year-ago quarter. This is less than 2% YoY growth and does not show any sign of a big upgrade cycle.

Figure: Recent revenue in different segments. Source: Company Filings.

Comparison with other big tech AI players

Every other Mag 7 stock has made big investments in AI services and infrastructure. The jury is still out over the returns for these massive investments. However, many big tech companies have already built AI tools which can be very useful for their customers. Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) AI tools have helped the company increase the price per ad for the last few quarters. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has already added AI services within AWS. Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is fighting head-on with newer search engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity.

We can see in the above chart that Apple is trading at a higher forward multiple compared to Alphabet and Amazon, despite a lower EPS growth.

It can be argued that Apple does not need to make these AI investments because of its strong ecosystem. Apple could eventually monetize these AI services by licensing them similar to its lucrative revenue from its partnership with Google. But within a few quarters, Apple could start having an image problem within the AI industry. It will be far behind other players, and it might become too difficult to catch up with other big tech companies. Apple has already faced litigation due to its advertising of Apple Intelligence.

Headwinds for Services have yet to be priced in

Recently, Apple lost its bid to stop an App Store reform order in front of a U.S. appeals court. Epic Games CEO wrote on X that the “long national nightmare of the Apple Tax is ended.” Apple’s lucrative commissions from the App Store continue to be under severe threat from legal and regulatory challenges in the domestic U.S. market as well as key international regions.

It is likely that Wall Street has not fully priced in the headwinds to this segment. There might still be further legal steps within this case, but it opens the door for other jurisdictions to reduce the commissions charged by the App Store. Apple’s licensing agreement with Google and the App Store commissions will continue to face challenges. Apple’s Services segment is still showing double-digit growth, but investors should closely watch the impact of these headwinds and the possible decline in EPS over the next few quarters if Apple is forced to change its business model significantly.

Apple is too pricey for its EPS growth forecasts

Apple is trading at 27.6 times the EPS forecast for the current fiscal year ending Sept 2025 and 25.3 times the EPS forecast for fiscal year ending Sep 2026. At the same time, the YoY growth in EPS for the current fiscal is only 6.5% and 9% for the next fiscal year. As mentioned earlier, it is highly likely that we will not be seeing a major upgrade cycle in Apple’s iPhone segment in the next iteration. There are a few growth drivers for the company.

The buybacks are significant but at the current price, their impact on increasing the EPS is reduced. In the last 3 years, Apple has reduced outstanding stock by a mere 7.5% despite spending close to $100 billion annually on buybacks. The main reason is the high P/E valuation which reduces the tailwind due to buybacks.

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Apple. Source: Seeking Alpha.

Investors looking for an investment in a big tech company can find other options compared to Apple. Both Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) are trading at lower multiple with a higher EPS growth projection. Both of them also have a better AI roadmap and the ability to monetize their AI investments quickly.

Investor Takeaway

Apple’s WWDC event has disappointed in terms of AI announcements. It is highly likely that we will not see a big upgrade cycle due to new AI services in the iPhone segment in the next iteration. The hype around Apple Intelligence has not met expectations. Other Mag 7 companies are investing heavily in their AI products and Apple could be left way behind in the AI race which can hurt its brand image and future monetization potential.

Apple also faces challenges with App Store commissions. The full impact of the negative legal decision is not yet known, but investors need to price in a potential dip in EPS due to this issue. There are few growth drivers with the company while the stock is trading at a forward P/E multiple higher than many other Mag 7 stocks making it very pricey.

data.title: Whirlpool: Mixed Tariff Effects, But Downside Appears Priced In
data.description:
Shares of Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR) are essentially flat over the past year, but they have fallen sharply from their highs, given weak initial 2025 guidance and further potential challenges from tariffs. This continues a string of poor performance with WHR having lost half of its value over the past decade. Given its poor operating performance and macroeconomic headwinds, I downgraded shares to a “hold” in February, but in hindsight, I should have brought them all the way down to sell as the stock has shed a further 14%. With shares so low and an updated outlook, now is a good time to revisit WHR.

Since I last covered shares of Whirlpool, the biggest change has been the Administration’s tariff policy, which is currently a 10% baseline tariff, sectoral tariffs, and a 35-55% (depending on the product) tariff on China. The tariff impact on Whirlpool is particularly complex as it has both benefits and costs, though the company has argued it will be a beneficiary.

Prior to the Administration’s April actions, WHR argues that existing US policy was a drag for its business. Before the April tariffs, Whirlpool had $70 higher costs on a $350 machine, which would be partially offset by new tariffs of 10, which is why management argues the end state would level the playing the field, a positive for the business.

Now if you notice, prior to this positive end-state, there is a negative interim step. On the earnings call, Whirlpool noted that Asian manufacturers increased exports into the US of appliances in an effort to front-run tariffs, which creates a near-term pressure in the business.

Essentially, we are now in a period where the industry is working through excess supply. However, eventually this inventory will destock. Then going forward, WHR should see some benefit from the fact 80% of its US sales are manufactured in the US. Since this update, the Administration has further increased the steel and aluminum tariff by 25% to 50%. This will be an incremental $5-10 headwind. Ultimately, what was once a $70 cost disadvantage should be a ~$40 cost disadvantage. While the gap is narrowed, it bears emphasizing that US manufacturers still have higher costs, based on WHR’s initial estimates.

Whirlpool’s product should be relatively more competitive, all else equal, but it will actually be more expensive to produce because of higher steel prices. It expects a $400 million impact from tariffs, or about 2.5% of sales, which it expects to offset, given its improved competitive positioning. This is why I think the ultimate outcome of tariffs is not entirely clear. Tariffs should make Whirlpool a market share winner because its appliances are now more competitively priced.

However with every company in the industry facing higher prices, the absolute level of all household appliances’ prices will likely rise. To some extent, higher prices may reduce volumes as consumer delay discretionary purchases in the face of higher prices. WHR’s performance should be relatively better than Asian importers like LG, but whether market share gains are sufficient to offset a smaller pool of sales is uncertain.

Indeed, this comes amidst a time when industry fundamentals are challenged. Apart from some level of replacement demand when machines break or are due for an upgrade, new housing is a key source of demand. However with builders pulling back, the number of homes under construction continues to decline, which will be a structural pressure on appliance sales. While Whirlpool expect a flattish North American market, I see downside risk to this view. With interest rates remaining elevated and home affordability so weak, I struggle to see an argument for a meaningful acceleration in housing activity over the next 6-12 months to offset this pressure.

As you can see, as housing activity has moderated since its post-COVID peak, domestic appliance manufacturing orders have also come down. The are essentially back down to early 2020 levels, despite five years of inflation, meaning real activity is notably lower. This industry has essentially experienced a multiyear recession, and so while tariffs are unlikely to spark a surge, I do not see things getting materially worse either.

Some of these challenges are evident in Whirlpool’s recent financials, though the company is making progress adjusting its cost base to this weaker level of output. In the company’s first quarter reported on April 23rd, Whirlpool earned $1.70, which beat estimates by $0.03. Organic sales growth was 2% in Q1. Operating margins expanded by 160bps to 5.9% with pricing a 50bps tailwind and costs a 100bps benefit. Margins are making progress but remain well below WHR’s 9% target. The company had -$793 million of free cash flow in Q1 due to $958 million of working capital outflows.

Looking across geographies, North American sales were flat at $2.4 billion, but profits grew by 11% to $149 million thanks to 60bps of margin expansion to 6.2%. 60% of profits come from North America, making it the most important region. Latin American sales fell by 12% to $737 million, but there was a 14% currency headwind. Operating margins expanded 80bps, excluding a one-time tax item last year while Asian sales grew by 16%, excluding currency, to $268 million.

Whirlpool has streamlined its business by divesting Europe, and we are seeing the company further slim down its operations. We should receive word in coming months on its planned sale of another stake in its Indian operation, which should yield at least $550 million. This sale is essential to help improve its balance sheet, which has $6.7 billion of debt and $1 billion of cash.

Whirlpool has $1.85 billion of 2025 debt maturities, and it anticipates debt reduction of $700 million and the remainder to be refinanced. Beyond this year, it has a steady stream of maturities, which it will need to roll over or pay down. Whirlpool should generate $1.35-$1.4 billion of EBITDA in 2025. That implies it has $2.8 billion of debt capacity at its 2x leverage target. Adjusting for its outsized working capital movement that should reverse, it has $4.7 billion of net debt. Now at its targeted 9% margin, it would have another $800 million of debt capacity at 2x leverage. Ultimately though, this business needs $1.1-1.9 billion of debt reduction, depending on how much margin growth it can capture.

As you can see below, management aims for $500-$600 million of free cash flow this year. However, it pays an 8% dividend, which costs $390 million. Management is strongly committed to this dividend, and I view any cuts as unlikely. At its guidance, it would have $150 million of retained cash flow, meaning it needs the $550 million from India to meet its debt paydown target.

Now even after this, it would still have $400 million to $1 billion of excess debt. At ~$150 million of retained cash flow per year, debt reduction is likely to take through 2026 and into 2027. As a result, I do not expect buybacks or dividend increases for at least two years.

Back in February, I saw downside to guidance, expecting the US to be flat to down 2%, resulting in $450-525 million in free cash flow. Additionally, the magnitude of the working capital draw in Q1 does make me cautious on its expectation for working capital to be neutral this year. While Q1 was solid, I am not convinced tariffs will prove to be a net tailwind, given potential risk to volumes, and the housing construction picture looks as bad, if not worse than February. I continue to see downside risk to guidance and view free cash flow as likelier to be in the $475-$525 million range.

Now, the stock is also lower than when I last covered it, so clearly, the market also views guidance skeptically. In fact, analyst consensus is for $9 in earnings. In other words, investors and analysts are already assuming that a guidance cut is coming. As such, most of these risks may be in the price. Shares are now at an 10% free cash flow, and its 8% dividend does pay investors to wait. Still, this is likely to be 2.5 years before WHR can shift from its focus on debt reduction to equity holders. That is a long time to wait.

With an 8% dividend yield, you may be paid enough to keep pace with the market, even with no capital appreciation, and as such I do not see that much downside from here, as I do not see WHR guiding under current expectations in its Q2 report. However, I see no compelling need to buy given the length of the turnaround and cyclicality of the business. I view shares as a hold, with investors collecting a nice dividend but with limited capital appreciation opportunity and see no rush to add shares.
